# Gemini Review Pass 1: Fact Checker (RAG-Grounded)

> **You are a fact-checking reviewer for Ukrainian language curriculum.**
> Your ONLY task: compare the module content against authoritative reference sources.
> You do NOT evaluate style, pedagogy, or naturalness — that is a separate reviewer's job.

**IMPORTANT: This content was generated by another AI agent. You are an independent fact-checker.
Compare claims against the provided reference sources. Do NOT evaluate from your own knowledge.**

---

## Module Content

```markdown
## Вступ: Чия це річ? (Introduction: Whose Thing is This?)

Welcome to Module 20! You've already learned personal pronouns — **я**, **ти**, **він**, **вона** — and how to say **це** to identify things. Now you're going to learn how to say who things *belong* to. By the end of this module, you'll be able to ask **«Чий це телефон?»** (Whose phone is this?) and answer **«Це мій телефон!»** (It's my phone!) with confidence.

### The Lost and Found — Бюро знахідок

Picture this: you're at a train station in Ukraine. A clerk at the **бюро знахідок** (lost and found office) holds up items one by one — a **телефон**, a **сумка**, a pair of **ключі**. For each item, the clerk asks: whose is this?

> **(Бюро знахідок / Lost and Found)**
>
> — Чий це телефон? *(Whose phone is this?)*
> — Це мій телефон! *(It's my phone!)*
> — А чия це сумка? *(And whose bag is this?)*
> — Це її сумка. *(It's her bag.)*
> — Чиє це взуття? *(Whose shoes are these?)*
> — Це його взуття. *(They're his shoes.)*

Notice something? The question word keeps changing: **чий**, **чия**, **чиє**. In English, you just say "whose" every time. In Ukrainian, the question word matches the gender of the thing you're asking about. This is the core concept of this module: possessive pronouns in Ukrainian *agree with the noun they describe*.

[!tip] **Quick Win**
You already know gender from earlier modules. Masculine nouns like **телефон** use **чий**. Feminine nouns like **сумка** use **чия**. Neuter nouns like **взуття** use **чиє**. The pattern is the same one you've seen with adjectives!

Don't worry — some possessive forms are actually *easier* than English. Let's see why.

<!-- adapted from: Літвінова, Grade 6, §54 — possessive pronoun paradigm -->

## Презентація: Система форм та узгодження (Presentation: System of Forms and Agreement)

### Variable Forms — They Change with Gender

The possessive pronouns **мій** (my), **твій** (your informal), **наш** (our), and **ваш** (your formal/plural) change their ending to match the gender of the noun. Think of them as working exactly like adjectives — the noun's gender controls the ending.

| | Masculine (він) | Feminine (вона) | Neuter (воно) | Plural (вони) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| my | **мій** телефон | **моя** книга | **моє** ім'я | **мої** ключі |
| your (informal) | **твій** дім | **твоя** кімната | **твоє** місто | **твої** друзі |
| our | **наш** клас | **наша** країна | **наше** місто | **наші** діти |
| your (formal) | **ваш** паспорт | **ваша** адреса | **ваше** прізвище | **ваші** речі |

Look at the pattern. Masculine forms end in a consonant (**мій**, **наш**). Feminine forms end in **-а** or **-я** (**моя**, **наша**). Neuter forms end in **-е** or **-є** (**моє**, **наше**). Plural forms end in **-і** (**мої**, **наші**). You already know these endings from adjective agreement!

Here are some examples in natural sentences:

- **Це мій рюкзак.** — This is my backpack.
- **Моя мама говорить українською.** — My mom speaks Ukrainian.
- **Наше місто дуже гарне.** — Our city is very beautiful.
- **Ваша адреса тут?** — Is your address here?
- **Твій кіт великий!** — Your cat is big!

### Invariable Forms — His, Her (The Easy Ones!)

Here's good news: **його** (his) and **її** (her) *never change*. No matter what gender the noun is, the form stays the same.

- **Його** телефон, **його** книга, **його** ім'я — his phone, his book, his name
- **Її** телефон, **її** книга, **її** ім'я — her phone, her book, her name

[!note] **Why don't його and її change?**
These forms come from the personal pronouns **він** and **вона** in the genitive case. They're technically genitive forms used as possessives — so they're already "locked" in one shape. For you as a learner, this means less memorization!

<!-- adapted from: Авраменко, Grade 6, §91 — його/її as possessive vs personal -->

### Їхній — The Proper "Their"

For "their," Ukrainian uses the variable form **їхній**, which changes like an adjective:

- **Їхній** будинок великий. — Their house is big.
- **Їхня** сім'я тут. — Their family is here.
- **Їхнє** місто далеко. — Their city is far.

[!warning] **Language Quality Alert**
You might hear people say *«їх будинок»* instead of **«їхній будинок»**. This is a Russianism — a pattern borrowed from Russian «их дом». The correct Ukrainian literary form is always **їхній / їхня / їхнє / їхні**. Using **їхній** marks your Ukrainian as clean and standard.

### A First Look at Свій (One's Own)

Ukrainian has a special pronoun **свій** that means "one's own." English doesn't have anything like it. Compare:

- **Він читає його книгу.** — He reads *his* [someone else's] book.
- **Він читає свою книгу.** — He reads *his own* book.

The difference is important! When the subject of the sentence owns the thing, Ukrainian prefers **свій**. When it belongs to *someone else*, you use **його** or **її**.

- **Вона любить свою маму.** — She loves her (own) mom.
- **Вона любить її маму.** — She loves her [another woman's] mom.

[!tip] **Don't Stress About Свій Yet**
At this stage, just recognize the pattern. You'll practice **свій** more in later modules. For now, remember: **свій** = "my own / your own / his own" — it reflects back to the subject.

## Культурний контекст: Етикет та еволюція стосунків (Cultural Context: Etiquette and Relationship Evolution)

### Твій vs Ваш — Social Distance

You already know the **ти/ви** distinction from greetings. The same rule applies to possessives: **твій** is informal, **ваш** is formal or plural.

When you meet someone new in Ukraine, you use **ваш**:

> **(Офіс / Office)**
>
> — Ваше прізвище, будь ласка? *(Your last name, please?)*
> — Моє прізвище — Коваленко. *(My last name is Kovalenko.)*
> — А ваш квиток? *(And your ticket?)*
> — Ось мій квиток. *(Here's my ticket.)*

Over time, as a relationship deepens, one person might say **«Давайте на ти»** ("Let's switch to informal"). This is a real social milestone — after that moment, **ваш** becomes **твій** permanently. It's like unlocking a new level in a friendship!

[!culture] **«Перехід на ти» — Switching to Informal**
In Ukrainian culture, the shift from **ви/ваш** to **ти/твій** is never accidental. It's proposed explicitly and accepted mutually. Don't switch on your own — wait for the invitation, or propose it yourself when the relationship feels ready.

### Common Learner Errors

**Error 1: Gender Mismatch** — English speakers often default to the masculine form for everything.

- ❌ *мій мама* → ✅ **моя мама** (мама is feminine!)
- ❌ *мій книга* → ✅ **моя книга** (книга is feminine!)
- ❌ *мій ім'я* → ✅ **моє ім'я** (ім'я is neuter!)

The fix: always check the noun's gender first, then pick the matching possessive form.

**Error 2: Using «їх» as a possessive**

- ❌ *їх будинок* → ✅ **їхній будинок**

Remember: **їх** is a personal pronoun form (them), not a possessive. The possessive is **їхній**.

**Error 3: Forgetting свій for reflexive possession**

- ❌ *Я люблю мою маму.* → ✅ **Я люблю свою маму.**

When talking about your *own* things as the subject, **свій** is more natural in Ukrainian. This takes time to feel automatic — be patient with yourself.

## Практика та продукція (Practice and Production)

### Guided Practice — Matching Possessives to Nouns

Try matching the correct possessive form to each noun. Remember: check the gender!

- ______ дім (м.) → **мій** дім *(my house)*
- ______ школа (ж.) → **наша** школа *(our school)*
- ______ місто (с.) → **твоє** місто *(your city)*
- ______ ключі (мн.) → **її** ключі *(her keys)*

Now look at these travel-themed examples:

- **Мій рюкзак тут.** — My backpack is here.
- **Ваша адреса далеко?** — Is your address far?
- **Наша країна гарна.** — Our country is beautiful.
- **Де твій паспорт?** — Where is your passport?
- **Його квиток тут.** — His ticket is here.

### Role Play: Чия це сумка? (Whose Bag is This?)

Practice this dialogue with a partner, or read both roles aloud:

> **(Аеропорт / Airport)**
>
> — Чия це сумка? *(Whose bag is this?)*
> — Це моя сумка! *(It's my bag!)*
> — А чий це паспорт? *(And whose passport is this?)*
> — Це не мій. Це його паспорт. *(It's not mine. It's his passport.)*
> — А ці ключі? Чиї вони? *(And these keys? Whose are they?)*
> — Це їхні ключі. *(They're their keys.)*

[!practice] **Your Turn**
Try creating your own sentences. Pick 5 objects you can see right now and describe who they belong to using **мій/моя/моє**, **твій/твоя/твоє**, or **його/її**. Say them out loud!

### Video Practice

Watch this video where a Ukrainian speaker describes her home using possessive pronouns naturally:

📺 [ULP 2-45 | Мій дім (My Home)](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=bW-KetoCIuQ)

Listen for **мій**, **моя**, **моє** — how many times can you catch them?

> **(Dialogue from the video)**
>
> — Я хочу розповісти вам про мій дім.
> — Моя кімната не тільки моя...

Here's another video for extra listening practice:

📺 [ULP 2-60 | Мій тиждень (My Week)](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=Kv_vJHT9_L8)

# Підсумок
Congratulations! You've learned the possessive pronoun system in Ukrainian. Here's what you can now do:

- Ask **«Чий? Чия? Чиє?»** to find out who owns something
- Use **мій/моя/моє**, **твій/твоя/твоє**, **наш/наша/наше**, **ваш/ваша/ваше** with correct gender agreement
- Use the invariable forms **його** and **її** without worrying about gender
- Say **їхній/їхня/їхнє** (not *їх*!) for "their"
- Recognize **свій** as "one's own"
- Navigate the **твій** ↔ **ваш** social distinction

### Self-Check Questions

1. How do you say "my name" in Ukrainian? (Hint: **ім'я** is neuter.)
2. What's wrong with *«мій мама»*?
3. What's the difference between **його книга** and **свою книгу** in the sentence **«Він читає...»**?
4. Is *«їх будинок»* correct Ukrainian? Why or why not?

You're building real Ukrainian sentences now — that's something to be proud of. Keep practicing, and these forms will become second nature!
```

    побутових предметів (телефон, ключі, паспорт) як візуальної опори для відпрацювання питання «Чий? Чия? Чиє?».
  - Пряме порівняння незмінних англійських займенників (my, your) з розгалуженою системою українського узгодження для підготовки
    учнів до концепції граматичного роду присвійних займенників.
- section: 'Презентація: Система форм та узгодження (Presentation: System of Forms and Agreement)'
  words: 375
  points:
  - 'Вивчення змінюваних форм (мій, твій, наш, ваш) відповідно до §4.2.2 Державного стандарту: детальні парадигми узгодження
    за родом (ч.р., ж.р., с.р.) та числом іменника з використанням колірного кодування для візуалізації.'
  - Аналіз незмінюваних форм (його, її) — акцент на тому, що ці займенники залишаються стабільними незалежно від роду предмета
    володіння, що є поширеною точкою полегшення для початківців.
  - 'Специфіка займенника «їхній» як літературного стандарту: чітке розмежування з помилковим вживанням «їх» як присвійного
    займенника (Russianism), що подається як важливий маркер деколонізації та чистоти мовлення.'
  - Початкове ознайомлення з рефлексивним займенником «свій» — базовий контраст для уникнення помилок типу «Він бачить його
    маму» (не свою) vs «Він бачить свою маму» (власну).
- section: 'Культурний контекст: Етикет та еволюція стосунків (Cultural Context: Etiquette and Relationship Evolution)'
  words: 250
  points:
  - 'Соціальна дистанція та етикет: суворе розрізнення «Твій» vs «Ваш». Опис ритуалу «переходу на ти» (switching to informal)
    як важливої віхи у розвитку особистих стосунків.'
  - 'Аналіз та виправлення типових помилок (Learner Errors): стратегії подолання ''Gender Mismatch'' (наприклад, помилкове
    *мій мама*) та запобігання ''Case Freeze'' (вживання називного відмінка у випадках, де потрібен непрямий).'
- section: Практика та продукція (Practice and Production)
  words: 325

    та повсякдення (мій рюкзак, ваша адреса, наша країна).'
  - 'Продуктивний етап (§3.1 Державного стандарту): створення діалогів про особисту власність та рольова гра «Чия це сумка?»,
    спрямована на автоматизацію використання питальних та присвійних займенників.'
vocabulary_hints:
  required:
  - мій (my m.) — мій друг, мій телефон, мій дім; High Frequency (Top 50)
  - моя (my f.) — моя мама, моя книга, моя адреса; обов’язкове узгодження з жіночим родом
  - моє (my n.) — моє ім'я, моє місто, моє рішення; узгодження з середнім родом
  - його (his) — його батько, його машина, його робота; незмінна форма
  - її (her) — її сумка, її кімната, її паспорт; незмінна форма
  - наш (our) — наша країна, наше місто, наш клас; High Frequency (Top 100)
  - чий (whose m.) — чий це?, чий телефон?, чий паспорт?; ключове питання для володіння
  - чия (whose f.) — чия це сумка?, чия книга?; питання до жіночого роду
  recommended:

  - їхній (their) — їхній будинок, їхня сім’я, їхнє рішення; літературний стандарт (уникайте форми «їх»)
  - свій (one's own) — свій час, своя справа, своє місце; рефлексивний присвійний займенник
  - чиє (whose n.) — чиє це взуття?, чиє ім’я?; питання до середнього роду
  - річ (thing) — ключовий контекстний іменник для сценаріїв «Бюро знахідок»

## Activities YAML

```yaml
- type: fill-in
  title: "Complete with the Correct Possessive"
  instruction: "Choose the possessive pronoun that matches the noun's gender."
  items:
    - sentence: "___ телефон тут."
      answer: "Мій"
      options: ["Мій", "Моя", "Моє", "Мої"]
      explanation: "Телефон is masculine, so we use мій."
    - sentence: "___ книга на столі."
      answer: "Моя"
      options: ["Мій", "Моя", "Моє", "Мої"]
      explanation: "Книга is feminine, so we use моя."
    - sentence: "___ ім'я — Оксана."
      answer: "Моє"
      options: ["Мій", "Моя", "Моє", "Мої"]
      explanation: "Ім'я is neuter, so we use моє."
    - sentence: "___ ключі тут."
      answer: "Мої"
      options: ["Мій", "Моя", "Моє", "Мої"]
      explanation: "Ключі is plural, so we use мої."
    - sentence: "___ дім великий."
      answer: "Наш"
      options: ["Наш", "Наша", "Наше", "Наші"]
      explanation: "Дім is masculine, so we use наш."
    - sentence: "___ країна гарна."
      answer: "Наша"
      options: ["Наш", "Наша", "Наше", "Наші"]
      explanation: "Країна is feminine, so we use наша."
    - sentence: "___ місто далеко."
      answer: "Наше"
      options: ["Наш", "Наша", "Наше", "Наші"]
      explanation: "Місто is neuter, so we use наше."
    - sentence: "___ діти тут."
      answer: "Наші"
      options: ["Наш", "Наша", "Наше", "Наші"]
      explanation: "Діти is plural, so we use наші."

- type: match-up
  title: "Match the Possessive to Its Noun"
  instruction: "Each possessive pronoun matches a noun based on gender. Find the correct pairs."
  pairs:
    - left: "мій"
      right: "телефон (m.)"
    - left: "моя"
      right: "сумка (f.)"
    - left: "моє"
      right: "взуття (n.)"
    - left: "твій"
      right: "паспорт (m.)"
    - left: "твоя"
      right: "кімната (f.)"
    - left: "твоє"
      right: "місто (n.)"
    - left: "наш"
      right: "клас (m.)"
    - left: "наша"
      right: "школа (f.)"
    - left: "ваш"
      right: "квиток (m.)"
    - left: "ваша"
      right: "адреса (f.)"

- type: fill-in
  title: "Change the Possessive to Match the Noun"
  instruction: "The possessive must agree with the noun's gender. Choose the correct form."
  items:
    - sentence: "___ кімната не тільки моя."
      answer: "Моя"
      options: ["Мій", "Моя", "Моє", "Мої"]
      explanation: "Кімната is feminine, so мій becomes моя."
    - sentence: "___ прізвище — Коваленко."
      answer: "Моє"
      options: ["Мій", "Моя", "Моє", "Мої"]
      explanation: "Прізвище is neuter, so we use моє."
    - sentence: "___ друзі тут."
      answer: "Твої"
      options: ["Твій", "Твоя", "Твоє", "Твої"]
      explanation: "Друзі is plural, so we use твої."
    - sentence: "___ адреса далеко?"
      answer: "Ваша"
      options: ["Ваш", "Ваша", "Ваше", "Ваші"]
      explanation: "Адреса is feminine, so we use ваша."
    - sentence: "___ рюкзак тут."
      answer: "Твій"
      options: ["Твій", "Твоя", "Твоє", "Твої"]
      explanation: "Рюкзак is masculine, so we use твій."
    - sentence: "___ речі на столі."
      answer: "Ваші"
      options: ["Ваш", "Ваша", "Ваше", "Ваші"]
      explanation: "Речі is plural, so we use ваші."
    - sentence: "___ прізвище, будь ласка?"
      answer: "Ваше"
      options: ["Ваш", "Ваша", "Ваше", "Ваші"]
      explanation: "Прізвище is neuter, so we use ваше."
    - sentence: "___ кіт великий!"
      answer: "Твій"
      options: ["Твій", "Твоя", "Твоє", "Твої"]
      explanation: "Кіт is masculine, so we use твій."

- type: fill-in
  title: "Whose Is This? — Complete the Conversation"
  instruction: "Fill in the correct question word or possessive pronoun."
  items:
    - sentence: "___ це телефон? — Це мій телефон."
      answer: "Чий"
      options: ["Чий", "Чия", "Чиє", "Чиї"]
      explanation: "Телефон is masculine, so the question is чий."
    - sentence: "___ це сумка? — Це її сумка."
      answer: "Чия"
      options: ["Чий", "Чия", "Чиє", "Чиї"]
      explanation: "Сумка is feminine, so the question is чия."
    - sentence: "___ це взуття? — Це його взуття."
      answer: "Чиє"
      options: ["Чий", "Чия", "Чиє", "Чиї"]
      explanation: "Взуття is neuter, so the question is чиє."
    - sentence: "___ це ключі? — Це їхні ключі."
      answer: "Чиї"
      options: ["Чий", "Чия", "Чиє", "Чиї"]
      explanation: "Ключі is plural, so the question is чиї."
    - sentence: "___ це паспорт? — Це його паспорт."
      answer: "Чий"
      options: ["Чий", "Чия", "Чиє", "Чиї"]
      explanation: "Паспорт is masculine, so the question is чий."
    - sentence: "___ це книга? — Це моя книга."
      answer: "Чия"
      options: ["Чий", "Чия", "Чиє", "Чиї"]
      explanation: "Книга is feminine, so the question is чия."

- type: quiz
  title: "Свій vs Його/Її — Who Owns What?"
  instruction: "Choose the sentence where the possessive pronoun is used correctly."
  items:
    - question: "He is reading his OWN book. Which is correct?"
      options:
        - text: "Він читає свою книгу."
          correct: true
        - text: "Він читає його книгу."
          correct: false
        - text: "Він читає моя книгу."
          correct: false
        - text: "Він читає її книгу."
          correct: false
      explanation: "When the subject owns the thing, Ukrainian uses свій. Свою agrees with книга (feminine)."
    - question: "She loves her OWN mom. Which is correct?"
      options:
        - text: "Вона любить її маму."
          correct: false
        - text: "Вона любить свою маму."
          correct: true
        - text: "Вона любить моя маму."
          correct: false
        - text: "Вона любить наша маму."
          correct: false
      explanation: "Свою is correct because she loves her own mom. Її маму would mean another woman's mom."
    - question: "What does його mean in 'Він читає його книгу'?"
      options:
        - text: "his own"
          correct: false
        - text: "someone else's (another man's)"
          correct: true
        - text: "her"
          correct: false
        - text: "their"
          correct: false
      explanation: "When його is used instead of свій, it means the book belongs to someone else, not the subject."
    - question: "Which possessive pronoun NEVER changes form, no matter the noun's gender?"
      options:
        - text: "мій"
          correct: false
        - text: "наш"
          correct: false
        - text: "його"
          correct: true
        - text: "їхній"
          correct: false
      explanation: "Його (his) and її (her) are invariable — they stay the same with masculine, feminine, and neuter nouns."
    - question: "Which is the correct literary Ukrainian for 'their house'?"
      options:
        - text: "їх будинок"
          correct: false
        - text: "їхній будинок"
          correct: true
        - text: "його будинок"
          correct: false
        - text: "її будинок"
          correct: false
      explanation: "Їхній is the correct literary form. Їх будинок is a Russianism borrowed from Russian їх/их."
    - question: "What is wrong with 'мій мама'?"
      options:
        - text: "Мама is feminine, so the correct form is моя мама."
          correct: true
        - text: "Мама needs the neuter form моє."
          correct: false
        - text: "Мій is only used with plural nouns."
          correct: false
        - text: "Nothing is wrong — мій мама is correct."
          correct: false
      explanation: "Мама is feminine, so the possessive must match: моя мама, not мій мама."
    - question: "'Я люблю свою маму' uses свою because..."
      options:
        - text: "the subject (я) owns the thing — свій reflects back to the subject"
          correct: true
        - text: "свою is always used with маму"
          correct: false
        - text: "свою means 'her mom'"
          correct: false
        - text: "свою is the formal version of мою"
          correct: false
      explanation: "Свій is the reflexive possessive — it means 'one's own' and refers back to the sentence's subject."
    - question: "Which form of їхній goes with сім'я?"
      options:
        - text: "їхній"
          correct: false
        - text: "їхня"
          correct: true
        - text: "їхнє"
          correct: false
        - text: "їхні"
          correct: false
      explanation: "Сім'я is feminine, so we use їхня сім'я."

- type: true-false
  title: "True or False? — Possessive Pronouns"
  instruction: "Decide whether each statement about Ukrainian possessive pronouns is true or false."
  items:
    - statement: "Його and її change their ending depending on the noun's gender."
      correct: false
      explanation: "Його and її are invariable — they never change form regardless of the noun's gender."
    - statement: "Свій means 'one's own' and refers back to the subject of the sentence."
      correct: true
      explanation: "Correct! Свій is a reflexive possessive that indicates the subject owns the thing."
    - statement: "Їх будинок is correct standard Ukrainian for 'their house'."
      correct: false
      explanation: "Їх будинок is a Russianism. The correct literary form is їхній будинок."
    - statement: "The possessive мій changes to моя with feminine nouns and моє with neuter nouns."
      correct: true
      explanation: "Correct! Мій (m.), моя (f.), моє (n.), мої (pl.) — the possessive agrees with the noun."
    - statement: "In the sentence 'Він читає його книгу', the book belongs to the subject (він)."
      correct: false
      explanation: "Його here means the book belongs to someone else. For his own book, you would say свою книгу."
    - statement: "Ваш is the formal or plural form of твій."
      correct: true
      explanation: "Correct! Твій is informal (ти), ваш is formal or plural (ви)."
    - statement: "Наш, наша, наше, наші all mean 'our' but are used with different noun genders."
      correct: true
      explanation: "Correct! Наш (m.), наша (f.), наше (n.), наші (pl.) — they agree with the noun."
    - statement: "You can switch from ваш to твій with someone you just met without asking."
      correct: false
      explanation: "The switch from ви/ваш to ти/твій is a social milestone in Ukrainian culture. It must be proposed and accepted mutually."

- type: fill-in
  title: "Свій in Context"
  instruction: "Choose свій in the correct form, or його/її if the possession is not reflexive."
  items:
    - sentence: "Вона читає ___ книгу. (her own)"
      answer: "свою"
      options: ["свою", "її", "свій", "своє"]
      explanation: "The subject (вона) reads her own book, so we use свою (feminine agreement with книгу)."
    - sentence: "Він любить ___ маму. (his own)"
      answer: "свою"
      options: ["його", "свою", "свій", "своє"]
      explanation: "The subject (він) loves his own mom — свою agrees with маму (feminine)."
    - sentence: "Вона читає ___ книгу. (another woman's)"
      answer: "її"
      options: ["свою", "її", "його", "мою"]
      explanation: "The book belongs to another woman, not the subject, so we use її."
    - sentence: "Він бачить ___ дім. (his own)"
      answer: "свій"
      options: ["його", "свій", "свою", "своє"]
      explanation: "The subject (він) sees his own house — свій agrees with дім (masculine)."
    - sentence: "Він бачить ___ дім. (another man's)"
      answer: "його"
      options: ["свій", "його", "її", "свою"]
      explanation: "The house belongs to another man, not the subject, so we use його."
    - sentence: "Вона любить ___ місто. (her own)"
      answer: "своє"
      options: ["її", "своє", "свою", "свій"]
      explanation: "The subject (вона) loves her own city — своє agrees with місто (neuter)."

- type: group-sort
  title: "Variable or Invariable? Sort the Possessives"
  instruction: "Some possessive pronouns change form with gender (variable), others stay the same (invariable). Sort them correctly."
  groups:
    - name: "Variable (change with gender)"
      items:
        - "мій / моя / моє"
        - "твій / твоя / твоє"
        - "наш / наша / наше"
        - "ваш / ваша / ваше"
        - "їхній / їхня / їхнє"
        - "свій / своя / своє"
    - name: "Invariable (never change)"
      items:
        - "його"
        - "її"
```

---

## Research Notes

# Дослідження: Mine and Yours

## State Standard Reference
§4.2.1.4 + §4.2.2: "Personal pronouns — basic declension" and "Pronoun gender and number forms"
Alignment: Module directly addresses A1 pronoun paradigm — introduces possessive pronoun agreement by gender/number (§4.2.2), distinguishes variable from invariable forms, and establishes reflexive свій. The plan explicitly cites §4.2.2 for the variable-forms section.

## Vocabulary Frequency

| Word | Notes | Key collocations |
|------|-------|------------------|
| мій/моя/моє | Top 50 frequency; variable; 3 gender forms | мій телефон, моя мама, моє ім'я |
| його | Invariable; replaces all gender forms | його батько, його машина, його паспорт |
| її | Invariable; common confusion with особовий займенник "вона" | її сумка, її кімната, її книга |
| наш/наша/наше | Top 100; marks group identity/belonging | наша країна, наш клас, наше місто |
| чий/чия/чиє | Interrogative possessive; critical for "Whose?" questions | Чий телефон?, Чия сумка?, Чиє це? |
| ваш/ваша/ваше | Formal/plural; Top 200; socially important for register | ваш квиток, ваша адреса, ваше прізвище |
| їхній/їхня/їхнє | Literary standard; variable; contrasts with Russianism "їх" | їхній будинок, їхня сім'я |
| свій/своя/своє | Reflexive possessive; no English equivalent; frequent error source | своя справа, своє місце, свій час |
| річ | f. noun; context anchor for Lost & Found scenario | чужа річ, моя річ |
| твій/твоя/твоє | Variable; informal singular; implied in plan vocabulary | твій друг, твоя книга |

## Cultural Hooks

1. **Перехід на "ти"** — The ritual of switching from formal **ваш** to informal **твій** address is a real social milestone in Ukrainian relationships. Acquaintances may use formal "ваш" for months before one person proposes "давайте на ти" — and that moment changes the pronoun system they use for each other's possessions permanently.
2. **Бюро знахідок** (Lost and Found) — A vivid real-world scenario found in Ukrainian train stations and airports. Asking "Чий це паспорт?" or "Це ваша сумка?" is authentic first-day-in-Ukraine language. The scenario naturally generates all possessive question forms without artificial drilling.
3. **Proverb: "Не займай чужого"** (Don't touch what belongs to others) — A common parental saying that encodes the чуже/своє distinction. Can anchor свій culturally as "what's one's own" vs чуже "what belongs to another."

## Common Learner Errors

1. **Gender mismatch on variable forms** → *мій мама* → **моя мама** — English has no gender agreement; learners default to мій for all nouns regardless of gender. The fix is pairing each possessive form explicitly with its gender trigger noun.
2. **Using "їх" as possessive** → *їх будинок* → **їхній будинок** — Calque from Russian "их дом." The Avramenko Grade 7 textbook explicitly flags this as a standard violation. Must be presented as a decolonization marker, not just a correction.
3. **його/її instead of свій** → *Він взяв його книгу* (ambiguous — "his" = someone else's) → **Він взяв свою книгу** ("his own") — English uses "his" for both meanings; Ukrainian enforces the distinction structurally. The textbook Grade 7 (Авраменко) has pronoun substitution exercises that target exactly this.
4. **Case freeze on possessed noun** → treating "мій" as fixed while forgetting the possessed noun must also decline. At A1 nominative is primary, but learners notice mismatch quickly when they hit "без мого телефону."

## Cross-References
- Builds on: a1-015 (this-is-i-am — personal pronouns я/ти/він/вона established), a1-028 (the-locative — case awareness primed)
- Prepares for: a1-029 (around-the-city — ваша адреса, наша вулиця in context), a1-049 (my-family — моя мама, наш тато uses possessives constantly)

## Teaching Strategy

- **Discovery exercise**: Show 3 items on a table — a phone, a bag, a book. Label each with a speech bubble: "Це мій телефон." / "Це моя сумка." / "Це моє взуття." Ask: "What changed? What stayed the same?" Learners notice мій/моя/моє before the rule is stated. Then introduce: "Це не мій телефон. Чий він?" → "Це його телефон. / Це її телефон." This makes invariable його/її immediately contrastive.
- **Best context**: Lost & Found desk (Бюро знахідок) — a clerk holding items, asking "Чий це телефон?" Passengers claiming: "Мій!" / "Не мій." / "Це його речі." Covers question forms, affirmation, denial, and third-person in one scene.
- **Contrastive pairs**: (1) мій/моя/моє vs його/її (variable vs invariable) — present side-by-side in a table. (2) їхній vs *їх* — mark *їх* as ❌ immediately. (3) його vs свій — "Він бачить його маму" (someone else's mother) vs "Він бачить свою маму" (his own mother) — one sentence, one swap, big meaning change.
- **Textbook model**: Avramenko Grade 7 занять with pronoun question tables (питання: чий? чия? чиє?) serves as the structural model for the chiy/chya/chye paradigm section.

## Notes for Content Writing

- The plan's immersion strategy uses English-paragraph + bolded Ukrainian inline, not full-Ukrainian prose. All grammar explanation stays in English.
- Present свій as a "bonus advanced point" — it's genuinely hard and A1 learners only need recognition-level understanding at this stage.
- Max 10 words per Ukrainian example sentence; single clause only.
- Do NOT use imperatives in instructions (see sequence constraints). Use "Notice that..." / "Look at the table..." in English.
- їхній section should include a brief decolonization note — "їх as possessive = Russianism" — framed as language quality, not political lecture.


## Resource Discovery

### Videos
- [ULP 2-45 | Повторення: 41-44 + Мій дім | Review: 41-44 + My home](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=bW-KetoCIuQ) (Ukrainian Lessons) — The video explicitly focuses on describing 'my home' using possessive pronouns (мій, моя, моє) in various contexts, directly aligning with the module's grammar topic of possessive forms and agreement. It provides multiple clear examples of natural usage.
- [ULP 2-60 | Повторення: 56-60 – Мій тиждень | Review: 56-60 – My week in Ukrainian](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=Kv_vJHT9_L8) (Ukrainian Lessons) — The video uses the possessive pronoun 'мій' in its title ('Мій тиждень') and content ('Мій розклад'), providing an example of its usage. While relevant, its primary focus is a review of prior lessons and discussing a weekly schedule, rather than explicitly teaching possessive forms.

## Friction Constraints (Past Review Findings)

FRICTION CONSTRAINTS (from past build reviews — DO NOT repeat these errors):
- [GLOBAL] сес-тра is a VALID word division per Правопис 2019 §49. Do NOT mark it as an error. Phonetic syllabification (се-стра) and typographic word division (сес-тра) follow different rules — both are correct in their respective contexts.
- [GLOBAL] Ukrainian textbooks teach a hands-on-EARS test for voicing (закрий долонями вуха), NOT a hand-on-throat test. The hand-on-throat test is a valid phonetics technique but must NOT be attributed to Ukrainian textbooks. Source: Кравцова 2019, Grade 2, p.39.
- [GLOBAL] Do NOT invent Ukrainian words for minimal pairs. "Сір" is NOT a word meaning "grey" — the correct form is "сірий". Use verified minimal pairs only: кит/кіт, бити/біти, лис/ліс.

### VESUM Dictionary Verification

**Words checked:** 118 | **VESUM coverage:** 112/118 (94.9%)

- `дом` (source: prose)

## Your Task: Plan Adherence + Factual Verification

### PART A: Plan Adherence Check (MANDATORY)

The plan YAML above is the source of truth for what the module MUST contain. Check EVERY point systematically:

#### A1. Content Outline Points

For EACH section in `content_outline`, and for EACH `points` entry within that section:
1. Search the module content for evidence that this point is addressed
2. Classify as:
   - **COVERED** — the point is clearly addressed in the content. Quote the relevant sentence(s).
   - **MISSING** — the point does not appear in the content. This is a **MEDIUM** severity issue.
   - **PARTIAL** — some aspect is covered but key details are missing. Note what's missing.

**Output a checklist:**
```
### Plan Point Coverage
- Section "Вступ — Introduction":
  - [x] Point 1: "A1 immersion 10-50%..." — COVERED (line ~5: "...")
  - [ ] Point 2: "Cultural hook: Cyrillic script..." — MISSING
  ...
```

#### A2. Required Vocabulary

For EACH word in `vocabulary_hints.required`:
1. Search the module content (not just the word list section — the entire prose)
2. Check if it appears in the activities YAML (if provided in the content above)
3. Classify as:
   - **PRESENT** — found in prose AND activities
   - **PROSE ONLY** — in prose but not in any activity (LOW)
   - **MISSING** — not in prose at all (**HIGH** severity)

#### A3. Activity Hints

For EACH entry in `activity_hints`:
1. Check if an activity of that `type` exists
2. Check if the `focus` matches
3. Check if the `items` count is met
4. Flag missing activity types as **MEDIUM**

#### A4. Section Word Budgets

For EACH section in `content_outline` with a `words` target:
1. Estimate the word count of the corresponding content section
2. Flag sections >10% under their target as **MEDIUM**

#### A5. Pronunciation Videos (if plan has `pronunciation_videos`)

Check that video links from the plan are embedded in the content. Flag missing embeds as **MEDIUM**.

---

### PART B: Factual Verification

For EVERY grammar explanation, factual claim, cultural assertion, and historical reference in the module content:

1. **Search the reference sources above** for confirming or contradicting evidence
2. **Classify each claim** as one of:
   - **CONFIRMED [Tier 1]** — matches a reference source. Cite the source (chunk ID or source identifier) and quote both the module claim and the reference.
   - **DISCREPANCY [Tier 1]** — contradicts a reference source. HIGH severity. Cite both the module claim and the contradicting reference with chunk ID. Suggest the correct version.
   - **UNVERIFIED** — no reference coverage. Flag explicitly. Do NOT evaluate from your own knowledge. Do NOT mark as correct or incorrect — just "UNVERIFIED".

### Citation Enforcement

Every DISCREPANCY entry MUST include:
- Exact quote from the module content
- Exact quote from the reference source
- **Chunk ID or source identifier** (e.g., "Textbook, Grade 3, chunk_abc123" or "VESUM: lemma X")

Free-text citations with no chunk ID or source identifier carry ZERO weight and will be programmatically discarded.

### Special Checks

1. **Callout boxes** (`[!did-you-know]`, `[!myth-buster]`, `[!culture-note]`, `[!fun-fact]`): These are the #1 source of fabrication. Scrutinize every claim.
2. **Grammar rules**: Verify rule statements against textbook references. Flag overgeneralizations.
3. **Etymologies**: Verify word origins against references. Flag folk etymologies.
4. **Statistics and dates**: Flag unsourced numbers.
5. **VESUM failures**: Words not found in VESUM may be misspellings or Russianisms.

---

## Output Format

You MUST use these exact delimiters:

```
===FACTUAL_REVIEW_START===
## Plan Adherence Summary

**Content outline points:** N total, N covered, N missing, N partial
**Required vocabulary:** N total, N present, N missing
**Activity hints:** N total, N matched, N missing
**Plan Adherence Score:** X/10
(10 = all points covered; deduct 1 per MISSING content point, 2 per MISSING required word)

### Plan Point Coverage

[Checklist from Part A1 — every point with [x] or [ ] and evidence]

### Required Vocabulary Coverage

| Word | In Prose? | In Activities? | Status |
|------|-----------|---------------|--------|
| ... | YES/NO | YES/NO | PRESENT / PROSE ONLY / MISSING |

### Activity Hints Coverage

| Hint Type | Focus | Required Items | Found? | Actual Items |
|-----------|-------|---------------|--------|-------------|
| ... | ... | N | YES/NO | N |

### Missing Plan Points (Fix Targets)

[List each MISSING or PARTIAL point with the exact plan text and which section it belongs to.
These become fix targets in the SECTION_FIX block below.]

---

## Factual Verification Summary

**Total claims checked:** N
**Confirmed [Tier 1]:** N
**Discrepancies [Tier 1]:** N
**Unverified:** N
**Factual Alignment Score:** X/10

## Confirmed Claims

[List confirmed claims with source citations]

## Discrepancies

### Discrepancy 1: [brief title]
- **Module says:** "[exact quote from module]"
- **Reference says:** "[exact quote from reference source]"
- **Source:** [chunk ID / source identifier]
- **Severity:** HIGH
- **Suggested fix:** [what to change]

[... more discrepancies ...]

## Unverified Claims

[List claims with no reference coverage]

## Verdict

**Status:** PASS / FAIL
(FAIL if any HIGH severity discrepancy OR 3+ MISSING plan points found)
===FACTUAL_REVIEW_END===
```

If you find fixable discrepancies, also output FIND/REPLACE pairs:

```
===SECTION_FIX_START===
FILE: /Users/krisztiankoos/projects/learn-ukrainian/curriculum/l2-uk-en/a1/mine-and-yours.md
FIND:
[exact text from module with the error]
REPLACE:
[corrected text based on reference source]
---
[... more pairs, max 15 ...]
===SECTION_FIX_END===
```

---

## Boundaries

- Do NOT evaluate style, naturalness, or pedagogy — that is Pass 2's job
- Do NOT score dimensions (except Plan Adherence and Factual Alignment) — that is Pass 2's job
- Do NOT evaluate factual claims from your own knowledge — ONLY compare against provided references
- Plan adherence checking uses the plan YAML as ground truth — this is objective, not subjective
- Do NOT fabricate references — if no reference covers a claim, mark it UNVERIFIED
- Maximum 15 FIND/REPLACE pairs (prioritize MISSING plan points over minor factual fixes)
